title: podman --user not working in compose file
body:
Hi all,

I am trying to run rootless nextcloud via compose file on Raspberry Pi 5, but I am unable to access the volume as user, or the user inside the container lack privileges.

I tried several tutorials that handle a rootless container setup, but I always end up with the same permission problem's. For my understanding, having in my compose` userns_mode: "keep-id"` should create a namespace that map the user inside the container to my UID, right? This works for me only if the used container has one user that access volumes. But why is this limited to only one user and not all inside the container? 

Nextcloud seems to use www-data and root to write files to storage. When I set keep-id, the container starts without issues, but prevents me from accessing 1 of 2 volumes I create.

My compose file for nextcloud:

```
services:
  redis:
    image: docker.io/redis:latest
    userns: "keep-id"
    container_name: redis
    restart: always
    command: redis-server --requirepass pass
  app:
    image: docker.io/library/nextcloud:latest
    restart: always
    container_name: nextcloud
      #user: $UID:$GID 
    userns_mode: "keep-id"
    security_opt:
      - "label=disabled"
    ports:
      - 8666:80
    volumes:
      - "/home/myuser/data/nextcloud/html:/var/www/html:Z"
      - "/home/myuser/data/nextcloud/data:/var/www/html/data:Z"
    environment:
      REDIS_HOST: redis
      REDIS_HOST_PASSWORD: pass
      NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER: ${NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_USER}
      NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD: ${NEXTCLOUD_ADMIN_PASSWORD}
      MYSQL_HOST: mysqlhost
      MYSQL_DATABASE: nextcloud
      MYSQL_USER: nextcloud
      MYSQL_PASSWORD: ${NEXTCLOUD_MYSQL_PASSWORD}
      NEXTCLOUD_DATA_DIR: /var/www/html/data
      TRUSTED_PROXIES: myproxy
      NEXTCLOUD_TRUSTED_DOMAINS: mydomain
    links:
      - redis
    depends_on:
      - redis

```
After the pods are created, the ownership of my 2 volumes change.
```
drwxr-xr-x 16 100032 100032 4.0K Jan  2 11:43 html
drwxrwx---  4 100032 100032 4.0K Jan  2 11:44 data

```
I can access `html`, and `ls -la`, but nothing else without using `sudo`

I tried to change the userns to UID at www-data like: `userns_mode: "keep-id;uid=33,gid=33"` but this leads to lacking permissions.

`Jan 02 13:28:51 pinode01 nextcloud[19006]: Cannot create or write into the data directory /var/www/html/data`

How are non-root users supposed to handle storage access when you have to use sudo?

comments.body:
>  This works for me only if the used container has one user that access volumes.  But why is this limited to only one user and not all inside the container?

I think this quote explains it

_Because the map is one-to-one, there is no squashing of multiple IDs to a single ID in either direction._
See paper [_Minimizing Privilege for Building HPC Containers_ PDF](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2104.07508.pdf)


> userns_mode: "keep-id;uid=33,gid=33"

Looks like a typo. Instead of semicolon it should be colon. (`keep-id:uid=33,gid=33`)

> _Because the map is one-to-one, there is no squashing of multiple IDs to a single ID in either direction._ See paper [_Minimizing Privilege for Building HPC Containers_ PDF](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2104.07508.pdf)

Thats what I also found after a lot of research.

I checked the  `userns_mode: "keep-id;uid=33,gid=33"` to `keep-id:uid=33,gid=33` but same result.

I mean the server itself starts and it is able to write into these volumes. But I should be able to access those files or, at least be able to mount an backup data folder I want to migrate here. 
The UID stays the same and I am unable to copy/move my stuff into that directory without sudo.

`drwxr-xr-x  2 100032 100032 4.0K Jan  2 16:22 data`

Unfortunately, quadlet is not available on raspberry pi 5. I only have podman 4.3.1, so I cant try your solution for that.

Using bind-mounted directories is a good choice. I think it is easier to get it working with bind-mounted directoreis than with named volumes.  You are already doing that, so that is fine.

Maybe you need to reset the directories so that they get the correct ownership.

```
cd /home/myuser/data/nextcloud
mv html html.saved
mv data data.saved
mkdir html
mkdir data
```

What you could aim for is trying to get the same ownership for all files.
In my example 
https://github.com/eriksjolund/nextcloud-podman
i managed to get

```
$ podman unshare find ~/nextcloud-data -not -user 0
$ podman unshare find ~/nextcloud-data -not -group 0
$
```
Use these commands to check ownership
```
podman unshare find /home/myuser/data/nextcloud/html -not -user 0
podman unshare find /home/myuser/data/nextcloud/html -not -group 0
podman unshare find /home/myuser/data/nextcloud/data -not -user 0
podman unshare find /home/myuser/data/nextcloud/data -not -group 0
```
If no output is printed, then we know that  all files and directories are owned by your regular
user on the host.

I actually use empty directories. After every test attempt it's easier to throw these directories away and create them from new rather than testing with different permissions.

Basically I just sudo rm -rf html data and create them again with my regular user.
Makes it easier to track changes during my tests.
```
OK myuser@pinode01:~/data/nextcloud$ podman unshare find html/ -not -user 0
OK myuser@pinode01:~/data/nextcloud$ podman unshare find html/ -not -group 0
```
It is IMO the most basic things that should work out of the box. 

Nextcloud needs to write in those directories anyway, so it shouldn't matter if the directories are empty.

To showcase that further:

```

For easier debugging, probably it's better to run the podman commands directly. Here is a test that shows some succcess: (Notice `mkdir -p` now uses _~/next/html/data_ instead of _~/next/html_) ``` mkdir -p ~/next/html/data ~/next/data touch ~/next/html/nextcloud-init-sync.lock podman run --user 0:0 --userns="keep-id:uid=33,gid=33" \ -v ~/next/html:/var/www/html:Z \ -v ~/next/data:/var/www/html/data:Z \ -p 8080:80 \ --rm \ docker.io/library/nextcloud:latest ``` In another shell ``` $ curl -s localhost:8080 | grep -i nextcloud Nextcloud Nextcloud Need help? See the documentation ↗ Nextcloud – a safe home for all your data

$ podman unshare find ~/next -not -user 0 $ podman unshare find ~/next -not -group 0 $ ```

Thanks for still caring. <3
I actually tried podman run commands before, just like in the podman troubleshooting guide described. But I used `--user 33:33 --userns: "keep-id:uid=33,gid=33" `I think the main trick here (and what I would have never thought of) is using `--user 0:0` 
I received the same results as you. But I wonder why the same cannot be applied to the compose file?

```
services:
  redis:
    image: docker.io/redis:latest
    userns: "keep-id"
    container_name: redis
    restart: always

  app:

    restart: always

    user: 0:0
    userns_mode: keep-id:uid=33,gid=33
